Article.ArticleTitle: Application of a novel Musculoskeletal Ultrasound Sum Score (MUSS) in the follow-up of patients with juvenile idiopathic arthritis.

Abstract.AbstractText: To evaluate the novel Musculoskeletal Ultrasound Sum Score (MUSS) as a longitudinal marker of disease activity (DA) in patients with juvenile idiopathic arthritis (JIA) within a treat-to-target management approach.

Demographics, clinical, laboratory and ultrasound (B Mode, BM, Power Doppler, PD, scored 0-3, using the pediatric OMERACT score) findings were recorded over one-year follow-up in the PRO-KIND cohort. Ultrasound imaging of joints was individual to the patient, determined by the treating clinicians as routine, unaware of the study aims. MUSS (combination of highest single BM grade of all examined joints + highest single PD grade from all examined joints) was calculated (maximum score 6). Spearman correlations with clinical activity measures and ROC-derived MUSS cut-offs for clinical inactive disease were calculated using pooled visit-level data.

Thirty-three JIA patients were included (oligoarthritis: n = 12, median age 4.4 years; polyarthritis: n = 21, 5.3 years). Median baseline MUSS was similar between subtypes. MUSS values decreased significantly over follow-up, paralleling improvements in JADAS-10 and global assessment scores. MUSS correlated strongly with JADAS-10, physician and parent/patient global scores (ρ = 0.68-0.81; all p< 0.001) and with ESR in polyarthritis (ρ = 0.57; p< 0.001). ROC analysis identified a MUSS cut-off ≥1 as optimal for detecting active disease (specificity 100%, sensitivity 55%).

MUSS is a promising, feasible sonographic score for longitudinal monitoring of disease activity in JIA, showing strong correlations with clinical measures and excellent specificity for active disease at a cut-off ≥1. Validation in independent, prospective cohorts is warranted.
